reasoning: According to the November 1, 2025 FIDE rankings on Wikipedia, Magnus Carlsen is ranked #1 with a classical rating of 2839, holding a massive 26-point lead over #2 Hikaru Nakamura (2813). FIDE rankings update monthly, meaning the November 16 resolution date will perfectly align with the current November 1 list, heavily guaranteeing he remains #1 (barring Wikipedia vandalism or unprecedented mid-month adjustments). For the December 9 list (which reflects December 1 updates), the primary classical event in November is the 2025 FIDE World Cup. However, Carlsen, Nakamura, Caruana, and several other top players have withdrawn from it, meaning their classical ratings will effectively be frozen for the December list. For the final resolution date of February 7, 2026, rankings will incorporate results from any late December or January events (like Tata Steel Chess). To overtake Carlsen, a competitor would need to bridge a 26-point gap in one or two classical tournaments, an overwhelmingly difficult feat for a 2800+ player since draws against lower-rated opponents cause rating loss. Carlsen's most recent major classical event was Norway Chess in June 2025, and since a player only loses active status after 12 months with no classical rated games, he is safely immune from removal by February 2026. Therefore, Carlsen is exceedingly likely to retain the #1 ranking on all three dates.
